input.query_title: Random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions and their applications
input.query_abstract: Recent work on the structure of social networks and the internet has focussed attention on graphs with distributions of vertex degree that are significantly different from the Poisson degree distributions that have been widely studied in the past. In this paper we develop in detail the theory of random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions. In addition to simple undirected, unipartite graphs, we examine the properties of directed and bipartite graphs. Among other results, we derive exact expressions for the position of the phase transition at which a giant component first forms, the mean component size, the size of the giant component if there is one, the mean number of vertices a certain distance away from a randomly chosen vertex, and the average vertex-vertex distance within a graph. We apply our theory to some real-world graphs, including the world-wide web and collaboration graphs of scientists and Fortune 1000 company directors. We demonstrate that in some cases random graphs with appropriate distributions of vertex degree predict with surprising accuracy the behavior of the real world, while in others there is a measurable discrepancy between theory and reality, perhaps indicating the presence of additional social structure in the network that is not captured by the random graph.
input.hyp_generic_object: neutron cascades in a fissile assembly with an arbitrary neutron-multiplicity distribution per fission
input.hyp_abstract: Predicting whether a fissile assembly sustains a divergent neutron chain, and characterizing the statistics of subcritical chains for source-driven and safeguards applications, requires accounting for the full distribution of neutrons emitted per fission rather than its mean alone. We formulate the neutron cascade as a branching process in which each induced fission releases a random number of prompt neutrons drawn from an arbitrary multiplicity distribution, encoded in a probability generating function. Under the assumption that successive neutron generations are statistically independent, valid when spatial and energy correlations are weak, self-composition of the generating function locates the critical condition separating chains that almost surely terminate from those with finite probability of unbounded growth, recovering unit multiplication factor as the threshold. We derive the probability that a single initiating neutron induces a divergent chain, the mean and variance of the neutron count in successive generations, and the expected total number of fissions in a terminating chain, all as derivatives of the generating function at unit argument. The formalism naturally accommodates delayed-neutron emission and multiple fissile species with distinct yield spectra. Predicted multiplicity moments and chain-length distributions agree closely with correlated-counting measurements on subcritical configurations, while residual discrepancies track spatial leakage effects omitted by the point-kinetics assumption.
input.candidate_title: Dynamics of Rumor Spreading in Complex Networks
input.candidate_abstract: We derive the mean-field equations characterizing the dynamics of a rumor process that takes place on top of complex heterogeneous networks. These equations are solved numerically by means of a stochastic approach. First, we present analytical and Monte Carlo calculations for homogeneous networks and compare the results with those obtained by the numerical method. Then, we study the spreading process in detail for random scale-free networks. The time profiles for several quantities are numerically computed, which allow us to distinguish among different variants of rumor spreading algorithms. Our conclusions are directed to possible applications in replicated database maintenance, peer to peer communication networks and social spreading phenomena.
